title: The Macro Minute: Does the US labor market support the Fed’s revised reaction function?

Happy Tuesday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here at Darius Dale to
present our macro minute for Tuesday,
December 16th, 2025. As always, we'll
start with the executive summary from
today's latest morning note. So, let's
hop right in. Today's key macro question
is, does the US labor market support the
Fed's revised reaction function? The
short answer is yes. Investors should
anticipate dovish surprises in US
monetary policy throughout the first
half of 2026, i.e. prior to Powell's
replacement as Fed chair.
In terms of analytical nuance, US
nonfarm payrolls rose by 64,000 in
November following a revised 105,000
decline in October, while the U3
headline unemployment rate increased to
4.6% from 4.4% in September, marking the
highest reading since September of 2021.
The October nonfarm payrolls
contraction, which was the steepest
since December of 2020, reflected a
162,000 drop in federal government
payrolls as workers who accepted
deferred resignation packages from the
Trump administration were formally
removed.
The Bureau of Labor Statistics canceled
the October jobs report and merged the
October payrolls into the November
report due to the historically long
government shutdown, noting that while
an October unemployment rate cannot be
reconstructed from the household survey,
November or nonfarm payroll figures are
based on the separate establishment
survey with broad digital reporting by
firms.
The December jobs report is scheduled
for release in early January ahead of
the January 28th FOMC decision and is
expected to carry substantially greater
weight in shaping near-term monetary
policy decisions. Markets are currently
pricing 2.5 additional 25 basis point
rate cuts in 2026, exceeding the Fed's
median projection of one reduction.
Recall that the Fed delivered a third
consecutive rate cut last week to
bolster what Fed chair Jerome Powell
described as gradually cooling labor a
gradually cooling labor market facing
significant downside risks, including
the risk of a jobless recovery.
Elsewhere, Treasury Secretary Scott
Bessent told Fox Business that one or
two additional interviews for the next
Fed chair will occur this week, but
President Donald Trump expected to
announce a decision in early January.
Investors would do well to anticipate
this person will drag the Fed further
into the fiscal dominance regime.
Lastly, global asset managers are
entering the new year with historic
optimism across growth, equities, and
commodities according to Bank of
America's monthly survey conducted from
December 5th through December 11th.
Bullish sentiment as measured through
cash balances, equity exposure, and
global growth expectations climbed to
7.4 out of 10 in December, the strongest
reading in 4.5 years. Bank of America
strategist Michael Hartnett noted that
this level has appeared to only eight
times since 2000, including November
2010 through February 2011, and November
2020 through July 2021.
FYI, the S&P 500 rallied 16% from
November 1st, 2010 through its April
29th, 2011 pre-crash peak. Moreover, the
S&P 500 rallied 49% from November 1st,
2020 through its January 3rd, 2022
pre-crash peak.
Bitcoin rallied 1,340% from November
1st, 2010 through April 29th, 2011, and
it rallied 233% from November 1st, 2020
through January 3rd, 2022.
Prior to December 13th, around the horn.
With the liquidity cycle still improving
on a trend basis, this suggests five of
the six key macro cycles currently are
or will be explicit and/or extreme
tailwinds for risk assets over the next
year.
While historically crowded bullish
positioning may make it difficult for
the market to achieve escape velocity
over any short-term time horizon, the
risk of bubbles in stocks, gold, and
Bitcoin is not inconsequential as
investors seek to protect their wealth
from accelerated financial repression
and monetary debasement that our
research has been forecasting since our
inaugural investing during a fourth
turning regime presentation in the
summer of 2023.
As always, members of our global
investor community can trust that our
institutional grade risk management
overlays, KISS and Dr. Mo, will bar
portfolios navigate these emergent risks
better than investors attempting to
manage risk without systematic rules or
relying on fundamental research alone.
Transition to our 42 macro dashboard. As
always, we'll wrap up with a question
from our community. This one's titled
gold versus Bitcoin. It says, "Hi DD,
thanks for the continued awesomeness
that is KISS and Dr. Mo." So, thank you.
Appreciate the kind feedback. The
question reads, "Question, gold and
Bitcoin are often considered to be
inflation hedges in a portfolio.
However, KISS is currently and appears
to be correctly landing in a risk-on
position for gold versus a risk-off
position for Bitcoin. How does the model
arrive there? Obviously, we're not
looking at inflation metrics. Are there
technical indicators within each asset
class that we're looking at, too?" So,
excellent question, especially
particularly for someone who's new and
has not had a chance to review our KISS
FAQ, which you can review here using the
hamburger menu here in the top right.

exposures are how are they calculated,
what are the allocations, how are they
determined, all that good stuff. So, I
highly recommend if you're going to be
using KISS using KISS or Dr. Mo to
manage a significant portion of your of
your wealth,
you might want to take the 15 minutes
that it takes to just review this so
that you don't have to ask, you know,
you know, so so you don't understand. I
want people to understand the process
better. The more you understand a
systematic process better, the more you
understand its its its its strengths,
its weaknesses, and ultimately you're
able to dispassionately execute the
signals when the signals land in your
inbox or on your your your text message
device. You know, what we don't want is
a signal landing in your inbox or via
your
your text message you know,
in your text messages and you go, "Well,
I don't understand this, so I'm not
going to do it." And that's how type two
errors get caused in in your portfolio.
And as we discussed in recent weeks,
type two errors are the kind of errors
that destroy your financial future. Type
one errors are annoying. Sometimes you
have head fakes and whipsaws in any
trend following process as we
experienced in late November, but the
reality is those are small, incredibly
small prices to pay to keep your
portfolio always in position to be on
the right side of market risk from a
trend perspective.
The larger of the two prices to pay,
which we and many of the top
institutional investors have figured
out, are type two errors. The kind of
errors that say, "Well, I'm going to
fight the trend. I'm going to if the
market's breaking down, I'm going to
stay long cuz I think it's cheap or or I
think the
crash or a correction is over."
Vice versa, the market's breaking out
and I'm going to stay short or
underweight because I don't think it's
justified or I'm worried about valuation
or some this, that, and the other from
the macro bear porn on YouTube
community. They're really good at
tickling your amygdala, by the way.
That's what they do for a living and
that's why they have so many subscribers
and why the subscribers have so few
dollars in their accounts. So, getting
back into KISS here, in terms of how the
allocations of KISS are are determined
for those who may be new or unfamiliar,
you know, what we're doing here is a
completely systematic quantitative trend
following process. There are no
fundamental thoughts. There's no Darius
Dale overlay. There's no anything
impacting KISS other than what you see
here on the page as reflected by the
top-down risk management overlay and the
bottom-up risk management overlay. So,
just one quick
quick thing before we can get into
those. In terms of KISS, so for those
who may be new, KISS is a 60/30/10
trend following strategy that is
designed to expose investor portfolios
to productivity growth via the 60%
target allocation in stocks. It's
designed to outrun financial repression
via the 30% target allocation in gold,
and it's designed to outrun monetary
debasement via the 10% target allocation
in Bitcoin.
Um
As you as you know, I didn't say
anything about inflation or inflation
hedges, this or that, the other. That's
that's that stuff. In my opinion, I
don't think gold and Bitcoin are
inflation hedges. They're hedges for
monetary debasement and financial
repression. Sometimes monetary
debasement and financial repression
cause inflation, particularly when the
fiscal authority is is is implementing
expansionary fiscal policy, but it
doesn't always have to be the case. In
fact, for most of my career, you know,
from let's say 2009 all the way up until
2020, the early part of 2021, all the
financial repression and monetary
debasement that we experienced as
investors didn't necessarily lead to
inflation. It only really led to
inflation once the fiscal authority got
involved and we they were actually
monetizing widening budget deficits as
opposed to
you know, keeping that liquidity trapped
in the financial sector. So, just be
aware of that. Don't Don't, you know,
you know, I think what we do we provide
a tremendous amount of
high-end institutional grade education
in our on our platform, particularly
through the lens of our KISS FAQ, our
Dr. Mo FAQ, our fourth turning FAQ, our
glossary, which explains all the terms
and models that we use to keep investors
on the right side of market risk from a
from a distribution of probable
economic, market, and policy outcome
perspective. And then we have obviously
our macro class, which is a three-hour
course broken up into three parts that
essentially explains what everything we
think investors need to know about macro
risk management to be, you know, top
quality institutional investors. Now,
granted, you know,
you're not going to read you're not
going to you're not going to review the
macro class and become a buy-side PM.
That's not you shouldn't have that
expectation, but you should have the
expectation that if you master all the
insights that we provide in our KISS
FAQ, in our Dr. Mo FAQ, in our fourth
turning FAQ, in our glossary, and in our
macro class, that you will have the
macro risk management intellect and the
macro risk management emotional
education to survive on the buy side.
And that's really what matters. It's
about creating a positively skewed
return distribution in your portfolio so
that you can have better results over
that compound that from higher net asset
values across multiple four market
cycles. You know, the investors that are
smarter than the the
all investing is is a bunch of people
changing trading assets with for money.
That's all it really is. It's I have
this asset, you have money. I'm going to
give you the asset, you give me the
money, and then in the future I'm going
to give you the money and go get the
asset. That's all it is. It's a bunch of
people trading assets for money. And so,
the people who are smarter in those
transactions tend to win the
transaction. They don't always win the
transaction, but they are they have a
positive skew to the frequency of the
wins that they have in those
transactions than the people who are
less smart than those people. So, my I
would just suggest
if you're an investor,
it might behoove you to become smarter
than the people on the other side of
your trades. They're people like me,
they're people like our portfolio
clients, many of the top institutional
investors in the world.
Hello to all of you.
Those are the people that are on the
other side of your trades all the time.
So, it would behoove you if you want
better investment performance,
better results,
you want to retire on time and
comfortably. If you want those positive
things,
it would behoove you to study the
educational resources that we make
available. So, uh anyway, getting back
into uh KISS, in terms of how the KISS
uh
you know, the allocations are
determined. So, it you know, there's
only two things that impact KISS ever in
terms of the exposures that you feel uh
as a customer of ours or that I feel as
someone who uses KISS to manage my
entire liquid net worth. It's the
top-down risk management overlay, which
we use to incorporate volatility
targeting into the strategy, and it's
the bottom-up risk management overlay,
which we use to incorporate dynamic
position sizing into the KISS strategy.
And these are the two of the hallmarks
of of of institutional risk management
uh that we've you know, picked up on our
collaboration across the global
buy-side. These are the two things that,
you know, the top funds that are
charging two and 20 and three and 30,
particularly the ones that are charging
three and 30, this is what they're
doing.
And so, what we're trying to do is
emulate what they're doing because we're
smart enough to copy the people who are
having the most success and making the
most money in financial markets. A lot
of you guys are out there copying God
knows what. Bunch of people who have no
wealth or no clients charging them at
three and 30. If you're going to copy
someone in the financial markets, copy
the people charging them their clients
the most money for their investment
process. Duh.
If you're going to copy a basketball
player, copy Michael Jordan or Kobe
Bryant or Steph Curry or LeBron James.
Don't copy Darius Dale, the former Yale
offensive lineman. I can't play
basketball.
You get it?
Get it?
So, anyway, getting back into this.
So, in terms of how the top-down risk
management overlay incorporates
volatility targeting into the strategy,
uh when the market regime is in a
risk-on condition, i.e. Goldilocks or
reflation, then the target allocation
for stocks via the SPL YM ETF uh is
maxed out at 60%, and if the market
regime is in a risk-off condition, i.e.
inflation or deflation, uh the target
allocation is cut in half to 30%. And
the reason it's cut in half uh is we
based on our our backtests, we found
that the volatility levels for the
assets like stocks, for risk assets
broadly, tend to double uh in risk-off
market regimes, and you tend to see
Sharpe ratios decline by, you know, 50
to 75% uh in these risk-off market
regimes. So, we're automatically adding
that vol targeting overlay based on the
our market regime nowcasting process. Um
and then gold uh can perform pretty well
in any market regime, so its target
allocation is fixed at 30%. And then the
same dynamic with Bitcoin as it as it
relates to stocks is when the market
regime is in a risk-on condition, i.e.
Goldilocks or reflation, Goldilocks
being risk-on with the disinflationary
bias, reflation being risk-on with an
inflationary bias, uh then the target
allocation for Bitcoin is maxed out at
10%. And then if the market regime is in
a risk-off condition, i.e. inflation or
deflation, inflation is risk-off with an
inflationary bias, deflation is risk-off
with a disinflationary bias, then the
target allocation for the FBTC ETF is
cut in half. Again, factoring in the
volatility backtest that we have for our
market regime nowcasting process. Uh and
then finally, our bottom-up risk
management overlay, we use our
volatility-adjusted momentum signal for
each of these independent asset classes
to determine to incorporate that dynamic
position sizing. So, for example, if an
ETF is bullish from the standpoint of
volatility-adjusted momentum signal,
then the actual exposure, the exposure
that we feel in our portfolio, is 100%
of this target allocation. Or or
whatever the 100% of the target
allocation that is determined by the
top-down risk management overlay, sorry.
Uh if then if the ETF if the the market
if the stocks, gold, or Bitcoin are
neutral from the perspective of the
volatility-adjusted momentum signal,
then the actual exposure is only 50% of
the target allocation as determined by
the top-down risk management overlay.
And then finally, for um you know, for
if an if if
if the market is bearish from the
standpoint of our volatility-adjusted
momentum signal, then the actual
exposure is 0% of the target allocation.
So, zero times any number is going to be
zero, which means that that exposure uh
is raising cash. Anytime you have a
neutral uh exposure from stocks, gold,
or Bitcoin are neutral, the BAM signal
from the standpoint of stocks, gold, or
Bitcoin, the KISS signal system raises
cash and parks that cash in, you know,
you can use whatever you want for cash,
but you know, I use uh uh uh
floating rate Treasuries uh to kind of
eliminate kind of the the the funkiness
that we tend to see in these uh debt
ceiling dynamics. But you can use
T-bills, you can use money market fund
exposure, you can stick your money under
the mattress. I don't particularly don't
care, that's the easy part of investing.
The hard part of investing is vol
targeting and dynamic position sizing to
make sure that your portfolio is always
in position to maximize upside capture
in bull markets and minimize downside
capture in bear markets. And if you do
that over enough market full market
cycles, I guarantee you'll retire on
time and comfortably. So, uh we'll wrap
it up. Darius Dale here presenting our
macro minute for Tuesday, December 16th,
2025. That's all I got there today.
